source_packet.title: Trust the Batch, On- or Off-Policy: Adaptive Policy Optimization for RL Post-Training
source_packet.audience: Readers who know policy gradients and want to understand the paper's on-policy and off-policy formulation.
source_packet.central_question: Can the current batch quantify behavior-policy mismatch and set both policy-gradient weighting and off-policy regularization without a fixed clipping factor?

section_policy.sequence_rule: Begin with the paper's motivating question, establish the prior objective and terminology before the mechanism, connect the mechanism to original code, then separate experimental evidence from limitations.

paper_excerpts.text: The paper names a trust-region concern and an off-policy concern, argues that fixed clipping conflates them, and asks the current batch to measure behavior-policy mismatch through normalized effective sample size. Preserve ownership: PPO contributes fixed clipping; Fakoor et al.'s original P3O already sets its off-policy cap c to ESS and mixture coefficient lambda to one minus ESS; FeynRL formulates the corresponding normalized, per-token P3O objective for large-model post-training.
Equation 11 defines normalized ESS from per-token policy ratios. Equation 12 uses e_B in the score-function weight and the off-policy regularizer of Policy-on Policy-off Policy Optimization.
The experiments test clipping factor, sampling temperature, and BF16 Train plus FP8 Rollout. Table 7 reports exact held-out pass@1 values, including the late precision-mismatch result.

code_notes.text: The implementation computes normalized ESS on valid tokens, caps rho at ESS, detaches rho in the policy term, and weights behavioral KL by one minus ESS.

agent_input.claim_policy: Use the paper's exact terms. Attribute fixed clipping to PPO, the ESS-controlled on/off-policy structure to original P3O, and the normalized per-token formulation to FeynRL. Use exact numbers only with equation or table locators. Label figure readings as authors' reported findings. Do not rename P3O components or invent motivational slogans.

concept_graph.thesis: FeynRL asks whether the current batch can measure behavior-policy mismatch through normalized effective sample size and use that statistic to set both policy-gradient weighting and off-policy regularization, instead of relying on a fixed clipping factor.

nodes.label: trust-region concern and an off-policy concern
nodes.summary: The paper separates two issues in RL post-training: limiting harmful policy updates and handling samples drawn from a behavior policy that may differ from the current policy. It argues that fixed clipping conflates these concerns.

nodes.label: on-policy score-function estimator
nodes.summary: Eq. 2 establishes the on-policy estimator before the paper changes the sampling distribution. This is the baseline thread for understanding why behavior-policy correction is later needed.

nodes.label: fixed clipping
nodes.summary: Eq. 4 shows the fixed clipping factor used by PPO-style objectives. In the paper's lineage, PPO contributes fixed clipping; this should not be attributed to FeynRL or original P3O.

nodes.label: behavior-policy samples
nodes.summary: Eqs. 5–7 move the expectation to behavior-policy samples and define the per-token policy ratio. This separates the sampling-policy change from the later adaptive control rule.

nodes.label: behavioral KL regularization
nodes.summary: Eq. 8 introduces an off-policy regularizer as a distinct ingredient from fixed clipping. This prepares the reader to see how P3O couples the score-function weight and behavioral KL coefficient.

nodes.label: Policy-on Policy-off Policy Optimization
nodes.summary: Fakoor et al.'s original P3O already sets its off-policy cap c to ESS and its mixture coefficient lambda to one minus ESS. FeynRL preserves this on/off-policy structure while adapting it to a normalized per-token formulation for large-model post-training.

nodes.label: Eq. 11 · normalized ESS
nodes.summary: Eq. 11 defines normalized ESS from per-token policy ratios. The statistic measures how concentrated the current batch's policy ratios are, using the current batch as the behavior-policy mismatch signal.

nodes.label: calculate_ess
nodes.summary: The confirmed implementation computes normalized ESS on valid tokens from per-token ratios across ranks, linking Eq. 11 to the training path rather than treating the formula as separate from code.

nodes.label: Eq. 12 · P3O objective
nodes.summary: Eq. 12 uses e_B in the score-function weight and the off-policy regularizer of Policy-on Policy-off Policy Optimization. One normalized ESS value controls both the capped score term and the behavioral KL weight.

nodes.label: compute_policy_loss
nodes.summary: The confirmed implementation forms the ratio, calls calculate_ess, caps rho at ESS, detaches rho in the policy term, adds behavioral KL weighted by one minus ESS, and reports ess_factor.

nodes.label: collect_rollouts / prepare_training_batches
nodes.summary: A prompt shard produces completions with rewards, z-scores, masks, and old log-probabilities; those tensors are prepared into training batches where the current policy can be compared with the behavior policy.

nodes.label: ReplayBuffer.add_batch_seqs / collate_fn
nodes.summary: The replay buffer stores aligned per-token tensors and collates padded micro-batches with masks, preserving which action-token positions contribute to ratio, ESS, and loss computation.

nodes.label: clipping factor
nodes.summary: Section 4 tests clipping factor. The authors report sensitivity across fixed clip choices for GRPO with ε ∈ {0.2, 0.4, 0.6}, while P3O does not require a clip-ratio choice.

nodes.label: sampling temperature
nodes.summary: Section 4 tests sampling temperature. The authors report that with Qwen3-4B and rollout temperature T=0.6 versus T=1.2, P3O remains robust while GRPO degrades at the larger mismatch.

nodes.label: BF16 Train plus FP8 Rollout
nodes.summary: Section 4 tests precision mismatch. Table 7 reports the BF16 Train + FP8 Rollout late result: GRPO falls from 0.499 to 0.029 between iterations 15 and 30, while P3O changes from 0.478 to 0.529.

nodes.label: two-anchor extension
nodes.summary: Eq. 13 separates behavior-policy mismatch from within-epoch policy drift. The appendix reports the extension can be stable at default temperature but becomes unstable at T=1.2, marking an important claim boundary.

edges.teaching_point: The paper's question is easiest to follow by first grounding the reader in the on-policy score-function estimator.
Fixed clipping enters as a PPO-style way to control updates, not as the paper's new adaptive mechanism.
The paper argues that fixed clipping conflates the trust-region concern with the off-policy concern.
Moving from on-policy samples to behavior-policy samples requires an explicit per-token policy ratio.
Behavioral KL regularization addresses behavior-policy mismatch separately from the fixed clipping rule.
Original P3O provides the ESS-controlled on/off-policy structure: cap c is ESS and mixture coefficient lambda is one minus ESS.
FeynRL's contribution is the normalized per-token ESS formulation for large-model post-training, not the invention of ESS-controlled P3O.
The normalized ESS e_B becomes the batch statistic used in both the score-function weight and the off-policy regularizer.
calculate_ess realizes Eq. 11 on valid per-token ratios, keeping the mathematical statistic linked to its training implementation.
compute_policy_loss realizes Eq. 12 by capping rho at ESS and weighting behavioral KL by one minus ESS.
Old log-probabilities, rewards, z-scores, masks, and response tokens must stay aligned so later ratio and ESS computations refer to the same valid action tokens.
The padded micro-batch and action-token masks define which positions contribute to normalized ESS.
The code path turns the batch mismatch statistic into the actual P3O loss terms used for backpropagation.
After the policy update and weight sync, the next rollout-policy version generates the next batch whose mismatch will be measured again.
The clipping-factor experiments evaluate the practical sensitivity of fixed clip choices against a P3O objective without a clip-ratio choice.
Changing rollout temperature increases behavior-policy mismatch, making it a direct test of the paper's off-policy motivation.
Precision mismatch creates another behavior-policy mismatch setting; Table 7 gives the exact held-out pass@1 comparison.
The reported clipping-factor results support the paper's claim that adaptive P3O avoids selecting a fixed clipping factor.
The authors' reported temperature-mismatch curves test whether ESS-controlled weighting and behavioral KL remain robust when the rollout distribution shifts.
The precision-mismatch table connects the mechanism to a late-collapse setting using exact reported pass@1 values.
The two-anchor extension shows that separating mismatch sources is possible, but the appendix-reported instability at T=1.2 limits the generality of that extension.

unresolved.summary: The packet confirms formula-to-code mappings for calculate_ess and compute_policy_loss, but does not provide confirmed code mappings for experimental evaluation scripts or Table 7 computation.
Figure-based findings should remain labeled as authors' reported curves; only Table 7 values should be treated as exact numeric results in this explainer.
The two-anchor extension is a claim boundary rather than the main confirmed mechanism: the appendix reports instability at T=1.2.

lesson_plan.title: Trust the Batch, On- or Off-Policy: Adaptive Policy Optimization for RL Post-Training
lesson_plan.promise: Explain how FeynRL formulates a normalized per-token version of Policy-on Policy-off Policy Optimization: PPO supplies fixed clipping, original P3O supplies the ESS-controlled on/off-policy structure, and FeynRL asks whether the current batch can measure behavior-policy mismatch through normalized effective sample size and use that statistic in the large-model post-training objective.

sections.title: Why ask the batch to measure behavior-policy mismatch?
sections.intent: Start with the paper's stated concern: fixed clipping is being used where the authors want to distinguish a trust-region concern from an off-policy concern. This section is needed before any equations because the later objective only makes sense as an answer to that separation problem.
sections.question: Can the current batch quantify behavior-policy mismatch and set both policy-gradient weighting and off-policy regularization without a fixed clipping factor?
sections.learning_goal: The reader should be able to state the paper's motivating distinction: fixed clipping controls update size, while behavior-policy mismatch arises because samples may come from a policy different from the current policy.
sections.misconception: Do not treat P3O as simply another fixed-clip PPO variant, and do not attribute PPO's fixed clipping to FeynRL.
sections.summary: FeynRL frames RL post-training around two concerns that fixed clipping can conflate: a trust-region concern and an off-policy concern. The paper's question is whether the current batch can measure behavior-policy mismatch directly through normalized effective sample size and use that measurement to adapt the objective.

medium.rationale: The cognitive job is framing the paper's question; no state change or controlled result transition is required.

sections.title: From the on-policy estimator to fixed clipping and behavior-policy samples
sections.intent: Establish the prior objective and terminology before introducing FeynRL's mechanism. The reader needs to see Eq. 2's on-policy estimator, PPO's fixed clipping in Eq. 4, the behavior-policy correction in Eqs. 5–7, behavioral KL in Eq. 8, and the original P3O ownership of the ESS-controlled on/off-policy structure.
sections.question: What exactly changes when the paper moves from on-policy samples and PPO-style fixed clipping to behavior-policy samples?
sections.learning_goal: The reader should be able to trace the lineage: Eq. 2 gives the on-policy score-function estimator; PPO contributes fixed clipping; Eqs. 5–7 move the expectation to behavior-policy samples using per-token policy ratios; Eq. 8 introduces behavioral KL; original P3O sets the off-policy cap c to ESS and the mixture coefficient lambda to one minus ESS.
sections.misconception: Do not say FeynRL invented the ESS-controlled P3O structure. FeynRL formulates a normalized, per-token P3O objective for large-model post-training.
sections.summary: The paper first grounds the objective in the on-policy score-function estimator, then contrasts it with PPO-style fixed clipping. It then changes the sampling distribution to behavior-policy samples and introduces per-token policy ratios and behavioral KL regularization. This prepares the central mechanism: original P3O already couples an ESS cap with a one-minus-ESS mixture coefficient, while FeynRL adapts that structure to normalized per-token large-model training.

medium.rationale: The equation transition carries meaning: the sampling policy changes, the per-token ratio appears, and behavioral KL is separated from fixed clipping.

sections.title: Eq. 11: normalized effective sample size as the batch mismatch statistic
sections.intent: Introduce the new control signal only after the reader knows why behavior-policy ratios are present. This section isolates the statistic: normalized ESS is computed from valid per-token policy ratios and measures ratio concentration in the current batch.
sections.question: How does FeynRL turn per-token policy ratios into a normalized measure of behavior-policy mismatch?
sections.learning_goal: The reader should understand that Eq. 11 defines normalized ESS from per-token policy ratios, and that the implementation computes it over valid action tokens rather than over padded positions.
sections.misconception: Do not interpret ESS as a learned reward, an advantage normalization, or a fixed hyperparameter. It is a batch statistic computed from current-versus-old policy ratios.
sections.summary: Eq. 11 defines normalized ESS using per-token policy ratios. When ratios are concentrated, the effective support of the batch is lower; when ratios are more uniform, the batch behaves as if it has more effective support. In the confirmed code path, calculate_ess computes this statistic on valid tokens across ranks.

medium.rationale: The state change matters: token count remains fixed while ratio concentration changes normalized ESS.

sections.title: Eq. 12: one e_B controls the score-function weight and behavioral KL
sections.intent: Use the ESS statistic to assemble the actual FeynRL P3O objective and keep the derivation linked to the code that realizes it. This section is needed after ESS because Eq. 12 depends on e_B as the shared control variable.
sections.question: How does normalized ESS enter Policy-on Policy-off Policy Optimization in the training loss?
sections.learning_goal: The reader should be able to explain that Eq. 12 uses e_B in both the score-function weight and the off-policy regularizer, and that compute_policy_loss forms ratios, calls calculate_ess, caps rho at ESS, detaches rho in the policy term, and weights behavioral KL by one minus ESS.
sections.misconception: Do not separate the code from the mechanism it realizes. The implementation is not a side note; it is where Eq. 11's batch statistic becomes Eq. 12's loss terms.
sections.summary: FeynRL's normalized per-token P3O objective uses the batch's e_B in two places: it caps the score-function weight and sets the behavioral KL coefficient through one minus e_B. The confirmed implementation follows that path by recomputing current log-probabilities, forming ratios against old log-probabilities, computing ess_factor, applying the capped detached rho term, adding the behavioral KL term, and reporting the ESS metric.

medium.rationale: The controlled transition carries meaning: as e_B changes, the score cap and behavioral KL weight move in opposite directions.

sections.title: Section 4: clipping factor, sampling temperature, and BF16 Train plus FP8 Rollout
sections.intent: After the objective is understood, test the paper's central claim under the experimental axes the authors chose. This section separates evidence from mechanism and labels figure readings as authors' reported findings.
sections.question: Do the reported experiments support replacing a fixed clipping factor with the batch-controlled P3O objective?
sections.learning_goal: The reader should be able to connect each experimental axis to the paper's concern: clipping factor tests sensitivity to fixed clip choices, sampling temperature stresses behavior-policy mismatch, and BF16 Train plus FP8 Rollout tests precision mismatch.
sections.misconception: Do not treat figure trends as independently recomputed measurements. Figure readings should be described as authors' reported curves; exact numeric claims should be limited to table-located values.
sections.summary: Section 4 tests fixed clipping choices, rollout temperature, and precision mismatch. The authors report sensitivity across fixed clip choices for GRPO while P3O does not require a clip-ratio choice. They also report robustness under higher sampling-temperature mismatch. For BF16 Train plus FP8 Rollout, Table 7 reports the late precision-mismatch comparison: GRPO falls from 0.499 to 0.029 between iterations 15 and 30, while P3O changes from 0.478 to 0.529.

medium.rationale: A controlled result transition carries meaning across the three experimental axes and Table 7 values.

sections.title: Eq. 13 and the two-anchor extension as a claim boundary
sections.intent: End by separating the main confirmed mechanism from the extension and its reported instability. This prevents the reader from overgeneralizing the paper's main P3O result to every possible mismatch decomposition.
sections.question: Where does the paper itself mark a boundary on the adaptive P3O story?
sections.learning_goal: The reader should understand that Eq. 13 separates behavior-policy mismatch from within-epoch policy drift, but the appendix-reported two-anchor extension is not the same as the main confirmed Eq. 11 to Eq. 12 mechanism.
sections.misconception: Do not present the two-anchor extension as an unconditional improvement. The appendix reports stability at default temperature but instability at T=1.2.
sections.summary: Eq. 13 introduces a two-anchor extension that separates behavior-policy mismatch from within-epoch policy drift. The appendix reports that this extension can be stable at default temperature but becomes unstable at T=1.2. This is an important claim boundary: the main FeynRL mechanism remains the normalized per-token ESS feeding the P3O objective, while the extension exposes a harder mismatch decomposition.

medium.rationale: The cognitive job is a boundary condition and interpretation warning; animation is not necessary.

blocks.label: Question
blocks.question: Can the current batch quantify behavior-policy mismatch and set both policy-gradient weighting and off-policy regularization without a fixed clipping factor?
blocks.context: FeynRL motivates its objective by separating a trust-region concern from an off-policy concern. The paper argues that PPO-style fixed clipping can conflate these concerns, while the later normalized per-token P3O formulation asks the batch itself to measure behavior-policy mismatch through normalized effective sample size.

blocks.heading: Why the batch is asked to measure mismatch
paragraphs: The paper’s starting concern is not simply that a clipping factor is hard to tune. It is that fixed clipping is being used in a setting where two different questions should be distinguished: how large an update should be, and how far the current policy is from the behavior policy that generated the samples.

In the paper’s framing, PPO contributes the fixed clipping mechanism. Original P3O already provides the ESS-controlled on/off-policy structure, with its off-policy cap tied to ESS and its mixture coefficient tied to one minus ESS. FeynRL’s stated contribution is to formulate this idea as a normalized, per-token P3O objective for large-model RL post-training.

columns.label: Trust-region concern
columns.question: What is being controlled?
columns.answer: The size or aggressiveness of the policy update, so the new policy does not move too far in one optimization step.

columns.label: Off-policy concern
columns.question: What is being measured?
columns.answer: Whether the valid tokens in the current batch were generated by a behavior policy that differs from the current policy used for the update.

columns.label: Fixed clipping
columns.question: Why is it not the paper’s final answer?
columns.answer: The paper argues that PPO-style fixed clipping can conflate update-size control with behavior-policy mismatch, because a fixed clip factor is not itself a batch measurement of mismatch.

columns.label: FeynRL / P3O direction
columns.question: What replaces the fixed clip choice?
columns.answer: The current batch supplies normalized effective sample size, which the later P3O objective uses for both policy-gradient weighting and off-policy regularization.

blocks.heading: Do not collapse the paper’s distinction back into clipping
paragraphs: The later equations make sense only after this separation is kept intact: fixed clipping belongs to the PPO-style prior objective, the ESS-controlled on/off-policy structure belongs to original P3O, and FeynRL gives the normalized per-token formulation used for large-model post-training.

So the motivating question is not whether FeynRL is another fixed-clip PPO variant. It is whether the current batch can provide the behavior-policy mismatch signal that sets both sides of the P3O objective.

blocks.label: Lineage question
blocks.question: What exactly changes when the paper moves from on-policy samples and PPO-style fixed clipping to behavior-policy samples?
blocks.context: The paper first grounds the objective in an on-policy score-function estimator, then contrasts that setup with PPO-style fixed clipping, and then moves the expectation to behavior-policy samples with per-token policy ratios and behavioral KL regularization. This lineage is needed before introducing FeynRL's normalized per-token P3O formulation.

blocks.heading: The paper separates two concerns that fixed clipping can conflate
paragraphs: The paper names a trust-region concern and an off-policy concern in RL post-training. Its related-work move is not to claim that fixed clipping or ESS-controlled P3O are new; instead, it uses those prior pieces to motivate a normalized, per-token P3O objective for large-model post-training.

In this lineage, PPO contributes the fixed clipping rule. Fakoor et al.'s original Policy-on Policy-off Policy Optimization already provides the ESS-controlled on/off-policy structure: the off-policy cap c is set to ESS and the mixture coefficient lambda is set to one minus ESS. FeynRL adapts that structure into the paper's normalized, per-token formulation.

nodes.label: Eq. 2 · on-policy score-function estimator
nodes.note: Starts with samples drawn from the current policy, so the estimator is the baseline before any behavior-policy correction is needed.

nodes.label: Eq. 4 · PPO-style fixed clipping
nodes.note: Introduces a fixed clipping factor as prior-work update control; the paper treats this as distinct from its adaptive batch statistic.

nodes.label: Eqs. 5–7 · behavior-policy samples and ratio
nodes.note: Moves the expectation to samples from a behavior policy and introduces the per-token policy ratio needed to correct that change of sampling distribution.

nodes.label: Eq. 8 · behavioral KL regularization
nodes.note: Adds a behavior-policy regularizer as a separate ingredient from fixed clipping.

nodes.label: Original P3O ownership
nodes.note: Original P3O already ties the off-policy cap c to ESS and the mixture coefficient lambda to one minus ESS.

nodes.label: FeynRL formulation
nodes.note: FeynRL formulates the corresponding normalized, per-token P3O objective for large-model post-training.

edges.teaching_point: The estimator establishes the on-policy baseline; fixed clipping is the PPO-style prior objective used for comparison.
When samples come from a behavior policy rather than the current policy, per-token policy ratios enter the objective.
Behavioral KL addresses behavior-policy mismatch separately from the fixed clipping rule.
The behavioral regularizer is the off-policy side that original P3O couples with ESS-controlled weighting.
FeynRL should be credited for the normalized per-token large-model formulation, not for inventing the ESS-controlled P3O structure.

blocks.heading: What changes in the transition
paragraphs: The central transition is the sampling distribution: Eq. 2 is on-policy, while Eqs. 5–7 rewrite the objective for behavior-policy samples and therefore require a per-token policy ratio. Eq. 8 then makes behavioral KL an explicit off-policy regularizer rather than hiding all concerns inside a fixed clipping factor.

This is why the ownership distinction matters. PPO contributes fixed clipping; original P3O contributes the ESS-controlled on/off-policy structure; FeynRL formulates the normalized, per-token P3O objective used for large-model post-training.

blocks.heading: ESS is the batch’s mismatch signal, not another reward term
paragraphs: The normalized ESS value e_B is high when the valid per-token ratios are comparatively uniform, meaning the current batch behaves as if it has more effective support under the current policy. It is lower when the ratios are concentrated, meaning fewer token positions effectively support the current-policy estimate.

In the paper’s formulation, this statistic is computed from current-versus-old policy ratios in the current batch. It should not be read as a learned reward, an advantage normalization, or a fixed hyperparameter.

blocks.heading: One control variable moves two loss terms in opposite directions
paragraphs: The key dependency is not just that P3O computes ESS; it is that Eq. 12 immediately spends the normalized ESS value twice. In the score-function part, e_B acts as the cap on the policy-ratio weight. In the off-policy part, one minus e_B sets the behavioral KL coefficient.

This preserves the paper's ownership of the pieces: PPO contributes the fixed clipping baseline, original P3O contributes the ESS-controlled on/off-policy structure, and FeynRL gives the normalized per-token formulation used for large-model post-training.

blocks.question: How does sampling-temperature mismatch affect training?

blocks.heading: What the evidence does—and does not—show
paragraphs: Across Section 4, the authors use three axes to stress the same concern from different directions: fixed clipping tests sensitivity to the PPO-style clip choice, sampling temperature changes behavior-policy mismatch, and BF16 Train plus FP8 Rollout creates a precision-mismatch setting with exact late checkpoint values in Table 7.

The evidence should be read with the paper's labels intact. Figure trends are authors' reported findings rather than independently recomputed measurements, and the exact numeric comparison in this section is the Table 7 precision-mismatch result.

blocks.label: Limits
blocks.question: Where does the paper itself mark a boundary on the adaptive P3O story?
blocks.context: The boundary is the paper’s Eq. 13 two-anchor extension: it separates behavior-policy mismatch from within-epoch policy drift, but the appendix-reported behavior of that extension should not be read as the same claim as the main Eq. 11 to Eq. 12 P3O mechanism.

blocks.heading: Eq. 13 is an extension, not the confirmed main mechanism
paragraphs: The main FeynRL mechanism is the normalized per-token effective sample size in Eq. 11 feeding the P3O objective in Eq. 12. Eq. 13 goes further by introducing a two-anchor extension that separates behavior-policy mismatch from within-epoch policy drift.

That separation is useful for interpretation, but it is also where the paper marks a limit: the extension exposes a harder mismatch decomposition rather than replacing the main normalized ESS-to-P3O mechanism.

appendix_entries.title: Paper question and terminology
appendix_entries.body: Sec. 1 and Sec. 3 frame the paper around a trust-region concern and an off-policy concern. The paper’s motivating question is whether the current batch can measure behavior-policy mismatch through normalized effective sample size and use that measurement in the later objective, rather than relying on a fixed clipping factor.

appendix_entries.title: Trust-region concern versus off-policy concern
appendix_entries.body: The source packet grounds the distinction as follows: the trust-region concern is about controlling harmful or overly large policy updates, while the off-policy concern is about samples that may come from a behavior policy different from the current policy. The paper argues that fixed clipping can conflate these two concerns and motivates a normalized per-token P3O formulation that uses batch-measured mismatch.

appendix_entries.title: Eq. 2 · on-policy score-function estimator
appendix_entries.body: Eq. 2 establishes the on-policy score-function estimator before the paper changes the sampling distribution. In the lineage, this is the baseline case: samples are drawn from the current policy, so the later behavior-policy correction has not yet entered.

appendix_entries.title: Eq. 4 · PPO-style fixed clipping
appendix_entries.body: Eq. 4 is the fixed-clipping prior objective used in the paper's comparison. The paper attributes this fixed clipping factor to PPO-style objectives and uses it to motivate the distinction between trust-region control and behavior-policy mismatch.

appendix_entries.title: Eqs. 5–7 · behavior-policy samples and per-token ratio
appendix_entries.body: Eqs. 5–7 move the expectation from current-policy samples to behavior-policy samples. That sampling-policy change introduces the per-token policy ratio between the current policy and the behavior policy.

appendix_entries.title: Eq. 8 · behavioral KL regularization
appendix_entries.body: Eq. 8 introduces behavioral KL regularization as an off-policy ingredient. In the related-work transition, it separates behavior-policy mismatch from the fixed clipping mechanism before the P3O structure couples the terms through ESS.

appendix_entries.title: Eqs. 9–10 · advantage-sign expansion of fixed clipping
appendix_entries.body: Eqs. 9–10 expand the fixed-clipping objective by advantage sign. They clarify how the fixed clipping rule acts for positive and negative advantages, but they do not add a new adaptive control variable in the lineage.

appendix_entries.title: Original P3O ownership of the ESS-controlled structure
appendix_entries.body: The paper preserves the ownership boundary: Fakoor et al.'s original Policy-on Policy-off Policy Optimization already sets the off-policy cap c to ESS and the mixture coefficient lambda to one minus ESS. FeynRL's contribution is the normalized, per-token P3O formulation for large-model post-training.

appendix_entries.title: Eq. 11 normalized ESS derivation
appendix_entries.body: Eq. 11 applies the effective-sample-size form to per-token policy ratios and normalizes by the number of valid token positions. The resulting e_B is a batch statistic: it is large when ratio mass is spread across the valid tokens and smaller when ratio mass is concentrated.

appendix_entries.title: calculate_ess code trace
appendix_entries.body: The confirmed implementation maps Eq. 11 to algs/P3O/p3o.py lines 107-135. The code path computes normalized ESS from valid per-token ratios, aggregates the needed quantities across ranks, and returns the ess_factor consumed by the policy-loss computation.

appendix_entries.title: Valid token masking for ESS
appendix_entries.body: The replay-buffer and collation path stores aligned per-token tensors and preserves masks for action-token positions. calculate_ess uses those valid positions for normalized ESS, so padded positions in a collated micro-batch do not become artificial evidence for the behavior-policy mismatch statistic.

appendix_entries.title: Eq. 12 P3O objective derivation link
appendix_entries.body: Eq. 11 defines normalized ESS e_B from per-token policy ratios. Eq. 12 then uses e_B in the score-function weight and uses one minus e_B for the off-policy behavioral KL regularizer. The packet also states the ownership chain: PPO supplies fixed clipping, original P3O supplies the ESS-controlled on/off-policy structure, and FeynRL gives the normalized per-token formulation for large-model post-training.

appendix_entries.title: compute_policy_loss realizes Eq. 12
appendix_entries.body: The confirmed code mapping for algs/P3O/p3o.py reports that compute_policy_loss forms the ratio, calls calculate_ess, caps rho at ESS, adds behavioral KL weighted by one minus ESS, and reports ess_factor. The relevant confirmed span is algs/P3O/p3o.py:192-250, with calculate_ess confirmed at algs/P3O/p3o.py:107-135.

appendix_entries.title: Rollout-to-loss data path
appendix_entries.body: The rollout path starts with prompt shards in collect_rollouts, produces completions with rewards, z-scores, masks, and old log-probabilities, stores aligned per-token tensors in ReplayBuffer.add_batch_seqs, collates padded micro-batches with valid action-token masks, and then compute_policy_loss recomputes current log-probabilities for the same completion tokens before ratio, ESS, and loss assembly.

appendix_entries.title: rho cap, detach, and behavioral KL
appendix_entries.body: The source packet confirms that the implementation computes normalized ESS on valid tokens, caps rho at ESS, detaches rho in the policy term, and weights behavioral KL by one minus ESS. This is the concrete code-side realization of Eq. 12's use of e_B in both the score-function weight and the off-policy regularizer.

appendix_entries.title: Section 4 experiment pipeline
appendix_entries.body: Section 4 varies one experimental factor at a time—clipping factor, sampling temperature, or rollout precision—then compares GRPO and P3O training behavior and reported held-out evaluation. The source packet describes the pipeline as rollout generation, objective update, training-reward tracking in Figures 1–3, and held-out pass@1 reporting in Table 7.

appendix_entries.title: Clipping-factor reported findings
appendix_entries.body: For the clipping-factor axis, the paper compares GRPO with ε ∈ {0.2, 0.4, 0.6} against one P3O objective. The packet states that the authors report sensitivity across fixed clip choices for GRPO, while P3O does not require a clip-ratio choice. Figure readings should remain labeled as authors' reported curves, not recomputed measurements.

appendix_entries.title: Sampling-temperature mismatch reported findings
appendix_entries.body: For the sampling-temperature axis, the paper compares Qwen3-4B rollout temperature T=0.6 versus T=1.2. The packet states that the authors report P3O remains robust while GRPO degrades at the larger mismatch. This finding is grounded in the authors' reported Figure 2 curves rather than independently extracted numeric values.

appendix_entries.title: BF16 Train plus FP8 Rollout Table 7 values
appendix_entries.body: For BF16 Train plus FP8 Rollout, Table 7 reports the exact late held-out pass@1 comparison: GRPO changes from 0.499 at iteration 15 to 0.029 at iteration 30, while P3O changes from 0.478 at iteration 15 to 0.529 at iteration 30. These are reported table values, not inferred from a plotted curve.

appendix_entries.title: Eq. 13 · two-anchor extension
appendix_entries.body: Eq. 13 is described as an extension that separates behavior-policy mismatch from within-epoch policy drift. In this explainer section it is treated as a claim boundary, not as the central confirmed Eq. 11 to Eq. 12 mechanism.

appendix_entries.title: Appendix Figs. 8–9 · reported stability boundary
appendix_entries.body: The appendix-reported curves for the two-anchor extension indicate stability at the default temperature but instability at T=1.2. This should be labeled as an authors-reported curve finding, not as an exact table value.

appendix_entries.title: Confirmed code scope for the limitation
appendix_entries.body: The provided code mapping confirms calculate_ess for Eq. 11 and compute_policy_loss for Eq. 12. The packet does not provide a confirmed mapping for an Eq. 13 implementation or for experimental evaluation scripts that compute Table 7.

formula_nodes.label: Eq. 2 — on-policy score-function estimator
formula_nodes.expression: Establish the estimator before changing the sampling distribution.

formula_nodes.label: Eq. 4 — fixed clipping
formula_nodes.expression: Show the fixed clipping factor used by PPO-style objectives.

formula_nodes.label: Eqs. 5–7 — behavior-policy correction
formula_nodes.expression: Move the expectation to behavior-policy samples and define the per-token policy ratio.

formula_nodes.label: Eq. 8 — behavioral KL regularization
formula_nodes.expression: Separate the off-policy regularizer from the fixed clipping mechanism before P3O couples them through e_B.

formula_nodes.label: Eq. 11 — normalized effective sample size
formula_nodes.expression: Compute the batch statistic that measures concentration of policy ratios.

formula_nodes.label: Eq. 12 — Policy-on Policy-off Policy Optimization
formula_nodes.expression: Use e_B in both the score-function weight and behavioral KL coefficient.

formula_nodes.label: Eq. 13 — two-anchor extension
formula_nodes.expression: Separate behavior-policy mismatch from within-epoch policy drift and expose the potential issue discussed by the authors.
